term_glossary.tooltip: Direct Liquid Cooling. A cooling approach where liquid removes heat close to heat-generating components such as processors or accelerators.
Coolant Distribution Unit. Equipment that pumps and conditions coolant and transfers heat between the TCS and facility systems.
Technology Cooling System. The closed secondary loop between the CDU and rack or server cooling hardware.
Facility Water System. The facility-side loop outside the TCS boundary.
A heat transfer fluid with 25 percent propylene glycol, commonly used in single-phase direct-to-chip cooling.
Quick Disconnect. A service connector that allows fluid connections to be made and broken in the loop.
Inorganic Additive Technology, often using phosphate, silicate, borate, or related inorganic corrosion inhibitors.
Organic Acid Technology, a coolant inhibitor approach associated with longer-life corrosion protection.
Hybrid Organic Acid Technology, combining organic acids with small amounts of inorganic chemistry.
A corrosion inhibitor family used to protect copper and yellow metals.
Total Suspended Solids. Particles suspended in the fluid.
Total Dissolved Solids. Dissolved material in the fluid, related to conductivity.
Colony Forming Units per milliliter, used for microbial counts in the fluid.
Inductively Coupled Plasma analysis, used to identify soluble ions and metal contamination.
Safety Data Sheet. A supplier document with hazards, handling, storage, PPE, and disposal information.
Certificate of Analysis. A manufacturer document showing product quality or conformance for a manufactured fluid.
Heat absorbed or released during a phase change, such as boiling or condensation.
Thermal Design Power. A processor or accelerator power and heat-design rating used when sizing cooling capacity; exact meaning can vary by vendor.

term_glossary.definition: American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers
term_glossary.tooltip: American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers. ASHRAE publishes data-center thermal guidance and terminology used by the course.
ASTM International. A standards organization whose test methods are commonly referenced for materials, fluids, and corrosion testing.
Quality Assurance. Testing and recordkeeping used to verify that fluid condition, supplier evidence, and operating data remain within expectations.
Personal Protective Equipment. Gloves, eye protection, and other controls selected from the SDS and local safety procedures.

slides.title: AI Heat Density Changes the Cooling Problem
slides.subtitle: Higher chip power and denser racks make the TCS fluid a reliability variable.

cards.title: Higher chip TDP
cards.body: Accelerators push heat beyond comfortable air-only limits.

cards.title: Denser racks
cards.body: More compute per rack increases the consequence of each loop decision.

cards.title: Residual air remains
cards.body: Liquid often carries most heat, but not every load in the rack.

cards.title: Uptime stakes rise
cards.body: Fluid problems can become compute availability problems.

figure.alt: Technology Cooling System loop between CDU and cold plates
figure.caption: The course focuses on the closed TCS loop between the CDU and the IT equipment.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

figure.alt: Treated water and PG25 are in scope while two-phase refrigerants are deferred
figure.caption: The actionable guidance in this course is for treated water and PG25 style single-phase loops.

slides.title: The Four Operator Questions
slides.subtitle: Fluid anxiety is practical: compatibility, leaks, monitoring, and expertise.

cards.title: Compatibility
cards.body: Will the fluid protect every wetted material?

cards.title: Leaks
cards.body: What happens when fluid appears where it should not?

cards.title: Monitoring
cards.body: What should be sampled, trended, and escalated?

cards.title: Expertise
cards.body: Who owns chemistry decisions during operation?

slides.title: Two-Phase Fluids Are a Different Course
slides.subtitle: Do not combine single-phase water/PG25 rules with two-phase refrigerant rules.

bullets.text: water or PG25 carries heat mainly as sensible heat and needs chemistry maintenance.
low-boiling dielectric refrigerants rely on latent heat and require pressure, vapor, and purge management.
compatibility discipline, SDS, COA, records, and fluid-to-system alignment.

figure.alt: Single-phase fluids use sensible heat while two-phase refrigerants use latent heat
figure.caption: Two-phase fluid education can be found in the two-phase DLC course.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks
Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

figure.alt: Standards and source map for DLC fluid guidance
figure.caption: Fluid guidance is a stack of community standards, test methods, and product-specific treatment plans.

slides.title: Module 1 Summary
items: The course focuses on single-phase TCS fluids: treated water and PG25.
Two-phase refrigerants need a separate learning path because their value comes from latent heat.
Fluid guidance is a lifecycle discipline, not a one-time procurement step.
Compatibility, monitoring, and records are the recurring themes.

slides.title: Check Your Fluid Boundary
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the fluid scope of this course?

choices.text: Airflow management inside traditional air-cooled racks.
Single-phase, water-based heat transfer fluids in direct-to-chip cold-plate loops.
Low-boiling-point dielectric refrigerants used for two-phase cold plates.
Only facility-side water treatment for building cooling towers.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. This course focuses on single-phase water-based fluids used in the technology cooling system for direct liquid cooling.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The scope is the single-phase water-based fluid used in the direct-to-chip cold-plate loop, not facility water or two-phase refrigerants.

questions.prompt: Which items belong in the operating questions for a single-phase fluid program?

choices.text: What fluid family and concentration are specified.
Whether low-boiling refrigerants can be run as single-phase coolants without changing flow needs.
Which chemistry limits and tests define fluid health.
Which materials, filters, and service practices touch the fluid.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Fluid selection, chemistry control, compatibility, filtration, and service practice all belong in the program.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The single-phase program is built around fluid choice, chemistry limits, wetted materials, filtration, and service practices.

slides.title: Choosing and Specifying Single-Phase Fluids
slides.subtitle: Water, PG25, chemistry windows, inhibitors, and wetted-material compatibility.

slides.title: Selection Decisions You Will Classify
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to...
objectives: Compare treated water and PG25 using performance and maintenance tradeoffs.
Identify the fluid quality targets that protect single-phase TCS loops.
Explain why inhibitor chemistry matters for corrosion control and deposits.
Connect fluid selection to wetted materials and supplier evidence.

figure.caption: Water favors thermal performance. PG25 adds biostatic operating margin when concentration is maintained.

slides.title: Ready-to-Use Fluid Quality Targets
slides.subtitle: Selected draft values for water-based heat transfer fluids.
rows: Particles and filter or microchannel risk
Easy field signal for dissolved material
Makeup water quality and scale risk

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Operating Temperature and Chemistry Window
slides.subtitle: Temperature is part of the compatibility requirement.

bullets.text: TCS loops are described as below 49 deg C in the draft guidance.
Operation is not expected to exceed 66 deg C without additional review.
Higher temperature requires review of wetted materials and treatment chemistry.
Confirm maximum surface stagnation temperature with IT and cold plate suppliers.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Corrosion Inhibitors: IAT, OAT, and HOAT

figure.alt: Coolant inhibitor chemistry timeline from water to IAT OAT and HOAT
figure.caption: Inhibitor strategy affects corrosion protection, service life, and contaminant sensitivity.

slides.title: Hard Water, Scale, and Deposit Risk

figure.alt: Hard water ions can form scale and threaten microchannels
figure.caption: Calcium and magnesium are small chemistry signals with large uptime consequences.
A material change is also a fluid-compatibility event.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Qualification and Supplier Evidence
slides.subtitle: The specification should be auditable after the purchase order is gone.

bullets.text: confirms manufactured product quality and conformance.
defines handling, hazards, PPE, storage, and disposal information.
sets local operating limits, tests, and corrective actions.
ties the fluid to wetted materials and operating conditions.
preserve decisions through hardware refresh cycles.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Module 2 Summary
items: Do not choose a fluid on thermal performance alone.
Fluid targets are operating signals, not paperwork.
Inhibitor chemistry affects corrosion, deposits, and monitoring.
The wetted materials list and operating temperature define compatibility.
COA, SDS, treatment plans, and records make the decision auditable.

slides.title: Check Your Fluid Selection
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What is the main tradeoff when choosing PG25 instead of treated water?

choices.text: PG25 always removes more heat than water at the same flow rate.
PG25 eliminates the need for compatibility evidence.
PG25 can add freeze protection and inhibitor package benefits, but it reduces heat capacity versus water.
PG25 turns the loop into a two-phase refrigerant system.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. PG25 can be useful, but lower heat capacity and fluid-property changes must be accounted for.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The tradeoff is not that PG25 is automatically better. It can help with freeze and inhibitor needs, while reducing heat capacity versus water.

questions.prompt: Which evidence belongs in a fluid qualification package?

choices.text: Only a generic fluid family name, with no concentration or material context.
Ready-to-use chemistry values such as concentration, pH, conductivity, and inhibitor limits.
Supplier guidance for operating limits, sampling, and maintenance response.
Compatibility evidence for wetted metals, polymers, elastomers, and connectors.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Qualification needs chemistry, material compatibility, and supplier operating guidance.
questions.feedback_incorrect: A generic fluid name is not enough. The package needs chemistry targets, exact materials, and supplier guidance.

slides.title: Commissioning, Filling, and Protecting the Loop
slides.subtitle: Serviceable design, leak checks, cleaning, filling, filtration, and microbial control.

slides.title: Commissioning Practices You Will Apply
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to...
objectives: Explain design features that make fluid service possible.
Describe clean startup and fill practices for new and existing systems.
Identify filtration choices that protect cold plate microchannels.
Recognize microbial-control practices and biocide cautions.

slides.title: Mechanical Design for Serviceability
slides.subtitle: A fluid program needs physical access to sample, add, drain, and isolate.

bullets.text: ports should support fluid replacement and controlled additions.
the loop should allow supplier-guided chemistry adjustments.
avoid deadlegs that trap contaminants or microbial populations.
feeders, reservoirs, bladders, and seals must follow the wetted-material list.
bleed-and-feed arrangements can support service while the loop remains online.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

figure.alt: Silent animation showing a new technology cooling system being flushed with cold plates isolated, flushed again with cold plates connected, and then filled with final fluid before sampling and recording a baseline.
figure.caption: A staged flush keeps construction debris away from cold plate microchannels before the final fluid fill and baseline sample.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: What Not to Put in the Loop
slides.subtitle: Uncontrolled cleaners and additives can turn into corrosion, residue, or deposit risk.

bullets.text: detergents and chemical cleaners require supplier guidance.
silicate can precipitate and deposit in microchannels.
consult municipal or regulatory requirements before draining.
do not introduce additives outside the approved treatment plan.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Startup Sampling and Baseline Records
slides.subtitle: The first clean sample is the reference point for future trend interpretation.

bullets.text: run the loop and check for leaks during final startup.
remove trapped air where needed before baseline sampling.
capture representative fluid from each TCS loop.
record unit, fluid identity, date, and startup condition.
retain baseline evidence for the life of the fluid.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

figure.caption: Filters should be easier to service than cold plate microchannels.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Microbial Control and Biofouling
slides.subtitle: Microbial control protects heat transfer, but biocide use must stay controlled.

bullets.text: keep operational total viable bacteria below 100 CFU/ml.
biocide treatment is highly recommended above 1000 CFU/ml.
use non-oxidizing biocide only if needed and per label.
chlorine and similar chemistry can create corrosion risk.
frequent additions can create organic or contaminant accumulation.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Module 3 Summary
items: Design the loop so fluid service can be done cleanly and safely.
Leak-check fluids and cleaning chemicals must be compatible with the final system.
Startup sampling creates the baseline for all later monitoring.
Sidestream filtration and microbial control protect cold plate microchannels.
Project Deschutes provides a useful filter-design example.

slides.title: Check Your Commissioning Discipline
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Which sequence best supports a clean new-system startup?

choices.text: Mix any available coolant with site water to save commissioning time.
Fill the system first, then decide whether cleaning is needed after several months.
Skip sampling because the fluid was new when shipped.
Clean and flush the loop, verify cleanliness, fill with approved fluid, then establish baseline records.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Startup should protect the loop before operation and create a baseline for future trend review.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Commissioning should clean and flush before fill, use approved fluid, and establish baseline records before normal operation.

questions.prompt: Which practices help protect the loop during service?

choices.text: Keep fill, flush, leak-response, and sample records.
Top off with unverified water or another fluid whenever volume is low.
Follow supplier guidance before adding biocide or other treatment.
Use compatible filters and serviceable locations for strainers or filters.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Serviceability, records, filtration, and controlled treatment protect the fluid and hardware.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Do not top off casually. Protect the loop with compatible filtration, records, and supplier-guided treatment decisions.

slides.title: Up Next - Monitoring and Operating for Fluid Health

motion_intro.title: Monitoring, Maintenance, and Operational Judgment
motion_intro.subtitle: Routine tests, lab QA, trend interpretation, adjustments, mixing, useful life, and documentation.

slides.title: Monitoring and Operating for Fluid Health
slides.subtitle: Testing schedules, trend interpretation, adjustments, mixing, and documentation.

slides.title: Operating Judgments You Will Make
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to...
objectives: Identify routine and laboratory fluid-health tests.
Use baseline and trend logic to interpret results.
Describe controlled adjustment and dosing practices.
Explain why mixed fluids complicate monitoring and correction.
Maintain records that support refresh cycles and troubleshooting.

slides.title: Routine Monitoring Schedule
slides.subtitle: The monitoring plan should become more intense when a parameter drifts out of spec.

cards.title: Startup
cards.body: test immediately after initial fill and circulation.

cards.title: Quarterly
cards.body: perform routine on-site checks during normal operation.

cards.title: Weekly when out of spec
cards.body: increase frequency until the parameter returns to range.

cards.title: Lab QA
cards.body: quarterly for one year, then six-month intervals if stable.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: On-Site Test Set
slides.subtitle: Routine tests are fast signals that guide escalation and lab follow-up.
rows: Compare with baseline and rate of rise
Retest or treat per supplier guidance

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Laboratory QA and ICP Analysis
slides.subtitle: Independent analysis helps distinguish fluid drift, contamination, and corrosion byproducts.
rows: Documents water quality changes over time
May not fully represent cold plate geometry

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks
Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Reading the Trend, Not Just the Number

figure.alt: Fluid health monitoring framework separating system health and fluid health
figure.caption: Use baseline and trend direction to distinguish system health from fluid health.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Adjustments and Supplier-Guided Dosing
slides.subtitle: Controlled dosing needs known volume, supplier guidance, circulation, retest, and records.

bullets.text: dosing cannot be calculated safely without loop volume.
adjust only according to treatment plan and technical support.
mix for 30 minutes before follow-up sampling.
confirm the adjustment moved results into range.
record date, system, additive type, amount, and reports.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

figure.caption: Mixing is best treated as a controlled event requiring fluid-specific evidence.

slides.title: Documentation and Submittals
slides.subtitle: If it changes the fluid, the materials, or the interpretation of a sample, record it.

bullets.text: source, COA, SDS, and analyses for each system.
required for calculating inhibitor or additive doses.
date, amount, additive type, and analytical reports.
metals, elastomers, plastics, filters, seals, and replacements.
operating parameters, testing schedule, and product documentation.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Water-based Heat Transfer Fluid in Single-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks

slides.title: Module 4 Summary
items: Use routine checks and lab QA together.
Interpret trends against baseline, not isolated values alone.
Make chemistry adjustments only through controlled supplier-guided procedures.
Treat fluid mixing as an event that changes monitoring complexity.
Maintain records that future operators can trust.

slides.title: Check Your Operating Judgment
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: What is the best way to interpret routine fluid-health results?

choices.text: React to a single number without checking method, history, or operating context.
Trend the results against baseline, limits, and recent changes before deciding on action.
Dose chemistry immediately whenever any value changes.
Ignore lab results when on-site test strips look acceptable.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Operating judgment comes from trend, baseline, limits, test method, and context.
questions.feedback_incorrect: A single value is not enough. Use trend, baseline, limits, test method, and operating context before acting.

questions.prompt: What should happen before mixing fluids or making chemistry adjustments?

choices.text: Use lab or on-site results to verify whether the adjustment worked.
Confirm supplier guidance and compatibility for the exact fluids and materials involved.
Assume all inhibited water-based fluids are interchangeable.
Document the change, date, fluid batch, concentration, and reason for action.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Mixing and dosing need supplier guidance, documentation, and follow-up verification.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Do not assume interchangeability. Confirm compatibility, document the action, and verify the result.
